coredata.dc:title: A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Qi
Xiong, Rui
Mu, Hao
Wang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The accuracy of lithium-ion battery model is one of the most important factors that affects the applicability of power battery in electrical vehicles. Based on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which, however, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters is performed by using the set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improved up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithion-ion battery
Parameter identification
Set-membership algorithm
Unknown but bounded noise

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 152 ( 8) 58 585 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems. 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.214 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.214 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Lo carbon cities and urban energy systems. 1876-6102 CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries Qi Jin, Rui Xio *, Hao Mu, Jun Wang Department of Vehicle Engineering, School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China. Abstract The accuracy of lithium-ion battery model is one of the most important factors that affects the applicability of power battery in electrical vehicles. Based on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which, however, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters is performed by using the set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improved up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithion-ion battery; Parameter identification; Set-membership algorithm; Unknown but bounded noise; 1. Introduction The battery pack, a crucial componen , affects the safe and reliable operation of electric vehicles, which reduces the environmental pollution and alleviates the energy shortage [1]. To ensure safe and effective operation of power battery under complex driving condition, thus, a battery management system(BMS) with a precise model is necessary * Corresponding author. Tel./Fax: +86 10 68914070;.E-mail address: rxiong@bit.edu.cn Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Sc enceDir ct Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights eserved. Selection and peer-revi w under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries Qi Jin, Rui Xiong*, Hao Mu, Jun Wang Department of Vehicle Engineering, School of Mechanical Eng neering, Beijing Institute of Technology, No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China. Abstract The accuracy of lithium-i battery m del is one of the most important factors that affects th applicability f power battery in electrical vehicles. Base on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subje ted to the norm l distribution of zero mean and zero covarian e, which, howev r, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery l t is perform by using th set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The mod l parameters are i entified by the set-membership algorith with the experim ntal data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal oltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improve up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithion-ion battery; Parameter identification; Set-membership algorithm; Unknown but bounded noise; 1. Introduction The battery pack, a crucial component, affects the safe and reliable operation of electric vehicles, which reduces the environmental pollut on and a l ates the energy shortage [1]. To ensure safe and effective operation of power battery under complex driving condition, thus, a battery management system(BMS) with a preci e mod l is necessary * Corresponding author. Tel./Fax: +86 10 68914070;.E-mail address: rxiong@bit.edu.cn 2 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 [2]. With the increased research in the field of electrical vehicles, numerous battery models have been proposed to simulate the character of batteries, such as equivalent circuit models, electrochemical models, etc. The equivalent circuit model(ECM) has few parameters and a good balance between the accuracy of the dynamic simulation and complexity [3]. However, the intrinsic parameters of battery are not measured directly by any devices, these variables can be identified by the model-based estimation algorithm. Although many methods are developed to estimate parameters, but the applicability, astringency and accuracy for ECM are still a research focus. Estimating parameters for battery has attracted much attention in the past few years. The recursive least squares(RLS) with an optimal forgetting factor is widely implemented for the online estimation of parameters [4-6], but the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. Whatâ€™s worse, it can generate large peaks at the stage of low SOC. Additionally, the adaptive extended Kalman filter(AEKF) algorithm is employed for the online parametersâ€™ identification [7], nevertheless, it also has the limitation that the noise needs to satisfy the Gaussian distribution. In this paper, an ellipsoidal outer-bounding set-membership estimation (EOB-SME) algorithm under the unknown- but-bounded(UBB) noise is adopted to identify parameters of power battery. The SM algorithm proposed an assumption that the unknown noise is bounded when priori knowledge of the noise is insufficient [8-9]. The boundary can be obtained by the model error, the sensor measurement error or etc. Simultaneously, the SM algorithm has the ability to identify redundant data and improve the robustness of the battery management system. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well. 2. Optimal Bounding Ellipsoid Algorithm Optimal bounding ellipsoid algorithm has wide applicability in problems involving identification and adaptive filtering. Set-membership identification is carried out under the assumption of UBB noise. The objective of the identification algorithm is to find a feasible solution set(FSS) in the parameter vector which is compatible with the measured data and the known noise boundary. Therefore, the OBE algorithm is different from the conventional methods, and the result is not a single point estimate, but a set of feasible parameter vectors, and each member in the set is a feasible solution. With the increasing of the sample size, the scope of membership set gradually shrinks. When the sample capacity tends to infinity, the membership set eventually converges to the real parameters of the system. OBE algorithm requires no statistical assumptions about model disturbances, for each time index k , the model error has a priori known boundary and satisfy the following bounded error condition. k kï�¥ ï�§ï‚£ (1) Considering the following model of the form k k k Ty ï�± ï�¥ï€½ï�† ï€« (2) in which nRï�± ïƒŽ is the unknown parameter vector to be estimated; nk Rï�† ïƒŽ is a measurable vector which is composed of known input and output; kï�¥ is a real but unknown error sequence which has a known boundary given by (1). Thus, a feasible solution set which is compatible with the measured data kï�† and the known noise bound kï�§ can be inferred at each time index k as follows ï�» ï�½nk k k k||| y ||TS Rï�± ï�± ï�§ï€½ ïƒŽ ï€­ï�† ï‚£ (3) Assuming that the priori information of parameter is 0ï�± ïƒŽï�‘ which is a bounded set in the parameter space. Given data on times [1, ]t kïƒŽ , then the feasible solution set is the intersection of 0ï�‘ and ï€¨ ï€©i 1,2, ,S i kï€½ . k 0 1 k i i FFS S ï€½ ïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ ï€½ ï�‘ ïƒ‡ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (4) Qi Jin et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 580â€“585 581 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries Qi Jin, Rui Xiong*, Hao Mu, Jun Wang Department of Vehicle Engineering, School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China. Abstract The accuracy of lithium-ion battery model is one of the most important factors that affects the applicability of power battery in electrical vehicles. Based on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which, however, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters is performed by using the set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improved up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithion-ion battery; Parameter identification; Set-membership algorithm; Unknown but bounded noise; 1. Introduction The battery pack, a crucial component, affects the safe and reliable operation of electric vehicles, which reduces the environmental pollution and alleviates the energy shortage [1]. To ensure safe and effective operation of power battery under complex driving condition, thus, a battery management system(BMS) with a precise model is necessary * Corresponding author. Tel./Fax: +86 10 68914070;.E-mail address: rxiong@bit.edu.cn Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights eserved. Selection and peer-revi w under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries Qi Jin, Rui Xiong*, Hao Mu, Jun Wang Department of Vehicle Engineering, School of Mechanical Eng neering, Beijing Institute of Technology, No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China. Abstract The accuracy of lithium-i battery m del is one of the most important factors that affects th applicability f power battery in electrical vehicles. Base on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subje ted to the norm l distribution of zero mean and zero covarian e, which, howev r, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery l t is perform by using th set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The mod l parameters are i entified by the set-membership algorith with the experim ntal data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal oltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improve up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithion-ion battery; Parameter identification; Set-membership algorithm; Unknown but bounded noise; 1. Introduction The battery pack, a crucial component, affects the safe and reliable operation of electric vehicles, which reduces the environmental pollution and alleviates the energy shortage [1]. To ensure safe and effective operation of power battery under complex driving condition, thus, a battery management system(BMS) with a precise model is necessary * Corresponding author. Tel./Fax: +86 10 68914070;.E-mail address: rxiong@bit.edu.cn 2 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 [2]. With the increased research in the field of electrical vehicles, numerous battery models have been proposed to simulate the character of batteries, such as equivalent circuit models, electrochemical models, etc. The equivalent circuit model(ECM) has few parameters and a good balance between the accuracy of the dynamic simulation and complexity [3]. However, the intrinsic parameters of battery are not measured directly by any devices, these variables can be identified by the model-based estimation algorithm. Although many methods are developed to estimate parameters, but the applicability, astringency and accuracy for ECM are still a research focus. Estimating parameters for battery has attracted much attention in the past few years. The recursive least squares(RLS) with an optimal forgetting factor is widely implemented for the online estimation of parameters [4-6], but the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. Whatâ€™s worse, it can generate large peaks at the stage of low SOC. Additionally, the adaptive extended Kalman filter(AEKF) algorithm is employed for the online parametersâ€™ identification [7], nevertheless, it also has the limitation that the noise needs to satisfy the Gaussian distribution. In this paper, an ellipsoidal outer-bounding set-membership estimation (EOB-SME) algorithm under the unknown- but-bounded(UBB) noise is adopted to identify parameters of power battery. The SM algorithm proposed an assumption that the unknown noise is bounded when priori knowledge of the noise is insufficient [8-9]. The boundary can be obtained by the model error, the sensor measurement error or etc. Simultaneously, the SM algorithm has the ability to identify redundant data and improve the robustness of the battery management system. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well. 2. Optimal Bounding Ellipsoid Algorithm Optimal bounding ellipsoid algorithm has wide applicability in problems involving identification and adaptive filtering. Set-membership identification is carried out under the assumption of UBB noise. The objective of the identification algorithm is to find a feasible solution set(FSS) in the parameter vector which is compatible with the measured data and the known noise boundary. Therefore, the OBE algorithm is different from the conventional methods, and the result is not a single point estimate, but a set of feasible parameter vectors, and each member in the set is a feasible solution. With the increasing of the sample size, the scope of membership set gradually shrinks. When the sample capacity tends to infinity, the membership set eventually converges to the real parameters of the system. OBE algorithm requires no statistical assumptions about model disturbances, for each time index k , the model error has a priori known boundary and satisfy the following bounded error condition. k kï�¥ ï�§ï‚£ (1) Considering the following model of the form k k k Ty ï�± ï�¥ï€½ï�† ï€« (2) in which nRï�± ïƒŽ is the unknown parameter vector to be estimated; nk Rï�† ïƒŽ is a measurable vector which is composed of known input and output; kï�¥ is a real but unknown error sequence which has a known boundary given by (1). Thus, a feasible solution set which is compatible with the measured data kï�† and the known noise bound kï�§ can be inferred at each time index k as follows ï�» ï�½nk k k k||| y ||TS Rï�± ï�± ï�§ï€½ ïƒŽ ï€­ï�† ï‚£ (3) Assuming that the priori information of parameter is 0ï�± ïƒŽï�‘ which is a bounded set in the parameter space. Given data on times [1, ]t kïƒŽ , then the feasible solution set is the intersection of 0ï�‘ and ï€¨ ï€©i 1,2, ,S i kï€½ . k 0 1 k i i FFS S ï€½ ïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ ï€½ ï�‘ ïƒ‡ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (4) 582 Qi Jin et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 580â€“585 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 Considering that the shape of the feasible solution set is generally undetermined or even nonconvex, hence, it is difficult to calculate it directly. The OBE algorithm adopts an ellipsoid to approximate the feasible set. The basic idea is to use the following ellipsoid to outsource the FFS. ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï�» ï�½1k k k k k k( ,P ) | 1TnFSS E R Pï�± ï�± ï�± ï�± ï�± ï�±ï€­ïƒ� ï€½ ïƒŽ ï€­ ï€­ ï‚£ (5) Among formula (5), kï�± , the center of the ellipsoid, is generally regarded as the result of the point estimation of the parameter; the envelope matrix kP determines the shape of ellipsoid and needs to satisfy the symmetric positive qualitative. In addition, the covariance matrix of the feasible solution set is 1k kC P ï€­ï€½ . Finally, the FSS can be solved by the iterative form of the optimal boundary ellipsoid method. 3. Model Parametersâ€™ Identification of a Lithium-Ion Power Battery Module 3.1. Establishment of battery model To model the dynamic behavior of the battery, a Thevenin model is built, shown in Fig.1. Fig. 1. The schematic of Thevenin model. Eqs. (6) and (7) are built to describe the electrical characteristics based on Kirchhoffâ€™s law. ocv t iD LU U U i Rï€½ ï€« ï€« (6) D D L D D U dUi C R dt ï€½ ï€« (7) where the ocvU is open circuit voltage, tU is terminal voltage, Li is the current, iR is the ohmic resistance, DR is the polarization internal resistance in the RC network, DC is the polarization capacitance, and the DU is the voltage of the RC network. Based on the Eqs. (6) and (7) , a Laplace transformation and a bilinear transformation are carried out to discretize the continuous system. And Eqs. (8) describes the discretization result. t 1 1 t 2 3( ) ( ) ( 1) ( 1) ( ) ( 1)OCV OCV L LU k U k aU k - aU k - a i k a i k -ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï€« ï€« (8) Define 1( ) ( ) ( 1)OCV OCVM k U k aU k -ï€½ ï€­ t 1 t 2 3( ) ( ) ( 1) ( ) ( 1)L LU k M k aU k - a i k a i k -ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« (9) ï€« ï€­ ï€« ï€­ ï€« ï€­ OCV UD RD CD Ut iL Ri ï‚· Ã— 4 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 1 2 3 2 22 2 2 2 i D i D D i D i D DD D D D D D D D R t R t R R C R t R t R R Ct R C a a a t R C t R C t R C ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€­ï�„ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï¼Œ ï¼Œ (10) Define (k) [1 U ( -1) ( ) ( -1)]t L Lk i k i kï�† ï€½ 1 2 3(k) [M(k) ]a a aï�± ï€½ (k) U ( ) ( ) ( )Tty k k kï�±ï€½ ï€½ï�† (11) Eq. (11) can be used directly by the OBE algorithm to identify the parameters. 3.2. Model Parametersâ€™ Identification Method The vector ( )kï�† is constructed based on the data of dynamic battery current and voltage. Then the initial value is assigned according to the convergence of the algorithm and the parameter characteristics of the battery. Normally, we can assign the (0)ï�† , (0)ï�± as 0; the covariance matrix (0)P can be assigned as 410 I , I is a n-dimensional unit matrix. Take the radius of the ellipsoid greater than zero, so the radius can be assigned as 2 (0) 1ï�³ ï€½ . The system noise threshold value ï�§ is determined according to the actual measuring equipment and modeling error. The feasible solution set can be deduced as follows: ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 1 1 11 1 T k k k P k k k k P k k k P k P k P k k k k G k ï�± ï�± ï�¢ ï�¤ ï�¢ ï�¡ ï�¡ ï�¢ ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï�† ïƒ© ïƒ¹ï€­ ï�† ï�† ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ï€«ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» (12) where ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©k y k k kï�¤ ï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† , ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 TG k k P k kï€½ ï�† ï€­ ï�† . In the Eqs. (12), the selection of ( )kï�¡ , ( )kï�¢ are related to the accuracy of the final identification results, therefore, they need to be solved according to ellipsoid optimization criterion[8],[12]. A schematic of the parametersâ€™ identification method as shown in Fig.2. 4. Verification In order to acquire data to identify the model parameters, the urban dynamometer driving schedule(UDDS) test on NCM battery is carried out. The test bench can see in paper [4]. The UDDS is a typical driving cycle which is often used to verify different battery models. The current and voltage profiles of the UDDS are shown in Fig.3. Fig. 3. (a) UDDS current profiles; (b) Voltage profiles of UDDS test 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 -40 -20 0 20 40 60 time(sec) I( A ) 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 2.5 3 3.5 4 time(sec) U (V ) (a) (b) Qi Jin et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 580â€“585 583 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 Considering that the shape of the feasible solution set is generally undetermined or even nonconvex, hence, it is difficult to calculate it directly. The OBE algorithm adopts an ellipsoid to approximate the feasible set. The basic idea is to use the following ellipsoid to outsource the FFS. ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï�» ï�½1k k k k k k( ,P ) | 1TnFSS E R Pï�± ï�± ï�± ï�± ï�± ï�±ï€­ïƒ� ï€½ ïƒŽ ï€­ ï€­ ï‚£ (5) Among formula (5), kï�± , the center of the ellipsoid, is generally regarded as the result of the point estimation of the parameter; the envelope matrix kP determines the shape of ellipsoid and needs to satisfy the symmetric positive qualitative. In addition, the covariance matrix of the feasible solution set is 1k kC P ï€­ï€½ . Finally, the FSS can be solved by the iterative form of the optimal boundary ellipsoid method. 3. Model Parametersâ€™ Identification of a Lithium-Ion Power Battery Module 3.1. Establishment of battery model To model the dynamic behavior of the battery, a Thevenin model is built, shown in Fig.1. Fig. 1. The schematic of Thevenin model. Eqs. (6) and (7) are built to describe the electrical characteristics based on Kirchhoffâ€™s law. ocv t iD LU U U i Rï€½ ï€« ï€« (6) D D L D D U dUi C R dt ï€½ ï€« (7) where the ocvU is open circuit voltage, tU is terminal voltage, Li is the current, iR is the ohmic resistance, DR is the polarization internal resistance in the RC network, DC is the polarization capacitance, and the DU is the voltage of the RC network. Based on the Eqs. (6) and (7) , a Laplace transformation and a bilinear transformation are carried out to discretize the continuous system. And Eqs. (8) describes the discretization result. t 1 1 t 2 3( ) ( ) ( 1) ( 1) ( ) ( 1)OCV OCV L LU k U k aU k - aU k - a i k a i k -ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï€« ï€« (8) Define 1( ) ( ) ( 1)OCV OCVM k U k aU k -ï€½ ï€­ t 1 t 2 3( ) ( ) ( 1) ( ) ( 1)L LU k M k aU k - a i k a i k -ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« (9) ï€« ï€­ ï€« ï€­ ï€« ï€­ OCV UD RD CD Ut iL Ri ï‚· Ã— 4 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 1 2 3 2 22 2 2 2 i D i D D i D i D DD D D D D D D D R t R t R R C R t R t R R Ct R C a a a t R C t R C t R C ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€­ï�„ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï�„ ï€« ï¼Œ ï¼Œ (10) Define (k) [1 U ( -1) ( ) ( -1)]t L Lk i k i kï�† ï€½ 1 2 3(k) [M(k) ]a a aï�± ï€½ (k) U ( ) ( ) ( )Tty k k kï�±ï€½ ï€½ï�† (11) Eq. (11) can be used directly by the OBE algorithm to identify the parameters. 3.2. Model Parametersâ€™ Identification Method The vector ( )kï�† is constructed based on the data of dynamic battery current and voltage. Then the initial value is assigned according to the convergence of the algorithm and the parameter characteristics of the battery. Normally, we can assign the (0)ï�† , (0)ï�± as 0; the covariance matrix (0)P can be assigned as 410 I , I is a n-dimensional unit matrix. Take the radius of the ellipsoid greater than zero, so the radius can be assigned as 2 (0) 1ï�³ ï€½ . The system noise threshold value ï�§ is determined according to the actual measuring equipment and modeling error. The feasible solution set can be deduced as follows: ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 1 1 11 1 T k k k P k k k k P k k k P k P k P k k k k G k ï�± ï�± ï�¢ ï�¤ ï�¢ ï�¡ ï�¡ ï�¢ ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï�† ïƒ© ïƒ¹ï€­ ï�† ï�† ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ï€«ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» (12) where ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©k y k k kï�¤ ï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† , ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 TG k k P k kï€½ ï�† ï€­ ï�† . In the Eqs. (12), the selection of ( )kï�¡ , ( )kï�¢ are related to the accuracy of the final identification results, therefore, they need to be solved according to ellipsoid optimization criterion[8],[12]. A schematic of the parametersâ€™ identification method as shown in Fig.2. 4. Verification In order to acquire data to identify the model parameters, the urban dynamometer driving schedule(UDDS) test on NCM battery is carried out. The test bench can see in paper [4]. The UDDS is a typical driving cycle which is often used to verify different battery models. The current and voltage profiles of the UDDS are shown in Fig.3. Fig. 3. (a) UDDS current profiles; (b) Voltage profiles of UDDS test 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 -40 -20 0 20 40 60 time(sec) I( A ) 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 2.5 3 3.5 4 time(sec) U (V ) (a) (b) 584 Qi Jin et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 580â€“585 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 Fig. 2. The schematic of the OBE parametersâ€™ identification method. Based on the previous discussion, an online model-based parameter estimation is conducted by the optimal bounding ellipsoid algorithm. The identification result is shown in Fig.4. Fig.4 shows the terminal voltage and its error between the experimental data and the model-based simulation data. It also demonstrates that the OBE algorithm can give good terminal voltage estimation, and the max error is less than 0.01V. Fig. 4. The terminal voltage and its error between the experimental data and the model-based simulation data ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 2 2 2 2 11 1 1 k k k k k k k k k G k ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¤ ï�³ ï�¢ ï�³ ï�¢ ï�§ ï�¢ ï�¢ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ ï€« ï€­ ï€­ ï€« ï€¨ ï€©2 min kï�³Optimatity criterion Input variables ï€¨ ï€©kï�† ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1k kï�¡ ï�¢ï€½ ï€­ ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 1 11 1 Tk P k k k P k P k P k k k k G k ï�¢ ï�¡ ï�¡ ï�¢ ïƒ© ïƒ¹ï€­ ï�† ï�† ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ï€«ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» Covariance matrix ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1k y k k kï�¤ ï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† ï€­ ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 TG k k P k kï€½ ï�† ï€­ ï�† ï€¨ ï€©P k ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©k kï�¡ ï�¢ã€� predict and update ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©Ë† Ë† 1k k k P k k kï�± ï�± ï�¢ ï�¤ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï�† ï€¨ ï€©P k Preditive voltage ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©y k k kï�±ï€½ ï�† ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 1 1tU k i k i kï€­ ï€­ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ« ïƒ» Voltage error ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©te k U k k kï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† ï€¨ ï€©kï�¢ ï€¨ ï€©n kï�† ï€¨ ï€©n kï�± Prameters ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 1 2 31 oc ocU k aU k a a aï€­ ï€­ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ« ïƒ» ï€¨ ï€©tU k ï€¨ ï€©1tU k ï€­ i ï€¨ ï€©i kï€¨ ï€©1i k ï€­ -1Z -1Z Optimize parameters 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 2 2.5 3 3.5 4 4.5 time(sec) U t(V ) SM RLS Test 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 -0.01 -0.005 0 0.005 0.01 time(sec) er ro r( V ) SM RLS11000 12000 3.55 3.6 3.65 20200 20230 -0.002 0 0.002 (a) (b) 6 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Table 1 shows the statistical analysis of estimated terminal voltage by the OBE algorithm, it is evident from the table 1 that the root mean square error of the terminal voltage is improved up to 8% compared with the RLS method, simultaneously, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%. Table 1. Statistical analysis of estimated results. Root-mean- square Mean value of absolute value Maximum value of absolute value SM 4.3277Ã—10-4 2.4767Ã—10-4 0.0077 RLS 4.7217Ã—10-4 2.7135Ã—10-4 0.0098 Comparison 8.3% 8.7% 21.4% Conclusion A novel method of parameter identification based on set-membership algorithm for lithium-ion battery is put forward in this paper. Based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results indicate the OBE algorithm gives more accurate terminal voltage estimation than RLS. By employing the online parameters identification approach based on the set-membership algorithm can identify redundant data and improve the robustness of the battery management system. Acknowledgements This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51707011), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 3182035) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No.2017YFB0103802). The systemic experiments of the lithium-ion batteries were performed at the Advanced Energy Storage and Application (AESA) Group, Beijing Institute of Technology. References [1] Zhang X, Lu J, Yuan S, et al. A novel method for identification of lithium-ion battery equivalent circuit model parameters considering electrochemical properties[J]. A UD factorizationâ€�based nonlinear adaptive setâ€�membership filter for ellipsoidal estimation[J]. International Journal of Robust & Nonlinear Control, 2008, 18(16):1513-1531. Qi Jin et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 580â€“585 585 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 Fig. 2. The schematic of the OBE parametersâ€™ identification method. Based on the previous discussion, an online model-based parameter estimation is conducted by the optimal bounding ellipsoid algorithm. The identification result is shown in Fig.4. Fig.4 shows the terminal voltage and its error between the experimental data and the model-based simulation data. It also demonstrates that the OBE algorithm can give good terminal voltage estimation, and the max error is less than 0.01V. Fig. 4. The terminal voltage and its error between the experimental data and the model-based simulation data ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 2 2 2 2 11 1 1 k k k k k k k k k G k ï�¢ ï�¢ ï�¤ ï�³ ï�¢ ï�³ ï�¢ ï�§ ï�¢ ï�¢ ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ ï€« ï€­ ï€­ ï€« ï€¨ ï€©2 min kï�³Optimatity criterion Input variables ï€¨ ï€©kï�† ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1k kï�¡ ï�¢ï€½ ï€­ ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 1 11 1 Tk P k k k P k P k P k k k k G k ï�¢ ï�¡ ï�¡ ï�¢ ïƒ© ïƒ¹ï€­ ï�† ï�† ï€­ ï€½ ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ï€«ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» Covariance matrix ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1k y k k kï�¤ ï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† ï€­ ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 TG k k P k kï€½ ï�† ï€­ ï�† ï€¨ ï€©P k ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©k kï�¡ ï�¢ã€� predict and update ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©Ë† Ë† 1k k k P k k kï�± ï�± ï�¢ ï�¤ï€½ ï€­ ï€« ï�† ï€¨ ï€©P k Preditive voltage ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©y k k kï�±ï€½ ï�† ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 1 1tU k i k i kï€­ ï€­ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ« ïƒ» Voltage error ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©te k U k k kï�±ï€½ ï€­ï�† ï€¨ ï€©kï�¢ ï€¨ ï€©n kï�† ï€¨ ï€©n kï�± Prameters ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€©1 1 2 31 oc ocU k aU k a a aï€­ ï€­ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ« ïƒ» ï€¨ ï€©tU k ï€¨ ï€©1tU k ï€­ i ï€¨ ï€©i kï€¨ ï€©1i k ï€­ -1Z -1Z Optimize parameters 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 2 2.5 3 3.5 4 4.5 time(sec) U t(V ) SM RLS Test 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 -0.01 -0.005 0 0.005 0.01 time(sec) er ro r( V ) SM RLS11000 12000 3.55 3.6 3.65 20200 20230 -0.002 0 0.002 (a) (b) 6 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Table 1 shows the statistical analysis of estimated terminal voltage by the OBE algorithm, it is evident from the table 1 that the root mean square error of the terminal voltage is improved up to 8% compared with the RLS method, simultaneously, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%. Table 1. Statistical analysis of estimated results. Root-mean- square Mean value of absolute value Maximum value of absolute value SM 4.3277Ã—10-4 2.4767Ã—10-4 0.0077 RLS 4.7217Ã—10-4 2.7135Ã—10-4 0.0098 Comparison 8.3% 8.7% 21.4% Conclusion A novel method of parameter identification based on set-membership algorithm for lithium-ion battery is put forward in this paper. Based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results indicate the OBE algorithm gives more accurate terminal voltage estimation than RLS. By employing the online parameters identification approach based on the set-membership algorithm can identify redundant data and improve the robustness of the battery management system. Acknowledgements This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51707011), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 3182035) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No.2017YFB0103802). The systemic experiments of the lithium-ion batteries were performed at the Advanced Energy Storage and Application (AESA) Group, Beijing Institute of Technology. International Journal of Robust & Nonlinear Control, 2008, 18(16):1513-1531. 20000 25000 -0.01 -0.005 0 0.005 0.01 time(sec) er ro r( V ) SM RLS11000 12000 3.55 3.6 3.65 20200 20230 -0.002 0 0.002 (a) (b) 6 Qi Jin, et al/ Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Table 1 shows the statistical analysis of estimated terminal voltage by the OBE algorithm, it is evident from the table 1 that the root mean square error of the terminal voltage is improved up to 8% compared with the RLS method, simultaneously, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%. Table 1. Statistical analysis of estimated results. Root-mean- square Mean value of absolute value Maximum value of absolute value SM 4.3277Ã—10-4 2.4767Ã—10-4 0.0077 RLS 4.7217Ã—10-4 2.7135Ã—10-4 0.0098 Comparison 8.3% 8.7% 21.4% Conclusion A novel method of parameter identification based on set-membership algorithm for lithium-ion battery is put forward in this paper. Based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results indicate the OBE algorithm gives more accurate terminal voltage estimation than RLS. By employing the online parameters identification approach based on the set-membership algorithm can identify redundant data and improve the robustness of the battery management system. Acknowledgements This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51707011), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 3182035) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant

head.ce:title: A set-membership algorithm based parameter identification method for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Vehicle Engineering, School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 5 South Zhongguancun Street, Haidian District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accuracy of lithium-ion battery model is one of the most important factors that affects the applicability of power battery in electrical vehicles. Based on the traditional forgetting factor recursive least square (FFRLS) method, the random noise should be subjected to the normal distribution of zero mean and zero covariance, which, however, is very difficult to be satisfied in practical application. In this paper, based on the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, the identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters is performed by using the set-membership identification algorithm with unknown but bounded noise. The model parameters are identified by the set-membership algorithm with the experimental data of UDDS test on the NCM battery module. Experiments and simulation results show that the new method can simulate the dynamics of battery well, it can keep terminal voltage error within 1%, alongside with the root mean square error(RMSE) improved up to 8% compared with the FFRLS, which verifies the feasibility and the effectiveness of the new method, as well as providing data support for accurate estimation of battery state.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel method for identification of lithium-ion battery equivalent circuit model parameters considering electrochemical properties[J]

Recursive modeling and online identification of lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicle applications[J]

Online model-based estimation of state-of-charge and open-circuit voltage of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles[J]
Energy
Online estimation of model parameters and state-of-charge of LiFePO 4, batteries in electric vehicles[J]
Applied Energy
optimization in optimal bounding ellipsoid algorithms[J]
IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Becis-Aubry Y, Boutayeb M, Darouach M. An optimal algorithm for the parameter identification of MIMO linear systems using bounding ellipsoids[J]. 2003, 5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bearing-only target tracking based on ellipsoidal outer-bounding set-membership estimation[J]
Beijing Hangkong Hangtian Daxue Xuebao/journal of Beijing University of Aeronautics & Astronautics
A New Nonlinear Set Membership Filter Based on Guaranteed Bounding Ellipsoid Algorithm[J]
Acta Automatica Sinica
A UD factorization‐based nonlinear adaptive set‐membership filter for ellipsoidal estimation[J]
International Journal of Robust & Nonlinear Control
